# Generated by google (gemini-2.5-flash)
# Prompt: Create Kong API Gateway configuration using declarative YAML. Define services and routes for microservices endpoints. Set up upstream targets and load balancing. This is for our API management platform.
# Category: kong_no_auth

_format_version: "3.0" # Or "1.1" for wider compatibility

# Define Upstreams for Load Balancing
# An Upstream represents a virtual hostname and can have multiple targets.
# Requests to a Service that uses this Upstream will be load balanced across its targets.
upstreams:
  - name: products-api-upstream
    slots: 1000 # Max number of targets this upstream can hold
    healthchecks:
      active:
        http_path: /health # Path to hit for health checks on targets
        timeout: 5 # Timeout for each health check request
        interval: 10 # How often to perform active health checks
        unhealthy:
          http_statuses: [400, 404, 500, 501, 502, 503, 504, 505]
          failures: 3 # Number of consecutive failures to mark a target as unhealthy
        healthy:
          http_statuses: [200, 201]
          successes: 2 # Number of consecutive successes to mark a target as healthy
      passive: # Passive health checks based on client traffic
        unhealthy:
          http_statuses: [500, 501, 502, 503, 504, 505]
          failures: 5
        healthy:
          http_statuses: [200, 201]
          successes: 5

# Define Targets for the Upstreams
# Targets are the actual backend instances that the Upstream will forward requests to.
targets:
  - upstream: products-api-upstream
    target: products-service-v1.internal:8080 # Hostname or IP and Port of the backend instance
    weight: 100 # Weight for load balancing (higher value gets more traffic)
    tags: ["v1", "production"]
  - upstream: products-api-upstream
    target: products-service-v2.internal:8080
    weight: 100
    tags: ["v2", "production"]
  - upstream: products-api-upstream
    target: products-service-v3.internal:8080
    weight: 50 # Lower weight, less traffic
    tags: ["v3", "canary"]

# Define Services
# A Service is an abstraction of an upstream API or microservice.
services:
  - name: users-service
    host: users-api.internal # The hostname/IP of your Users microservice
    port: 8080
    protocol: http
    path: /api/v1 # Base path for this service's endpoints
    retries: 5 # Number of retries on upstream failure
    connect_timeout: 60000 # Timeout for establishing a connection to the upstream server
    write_timeout: 60000 # Timeout for sending a request to the upstream server
    read_timeout: 60000 # Timeout for receiving a response from the upstream server
    tags: ["users", "core"]

  - name: products-service
    host: products-api-upstream # Host points to the defined Upstream for load balancing
    port: 8080 # The port that targets in the upstream are listening on
    protocol: http
    path: /
    retries: 5
    connect_timeout: 60000
    write_timeout: 60000
    read_timeout: 60000
    tags: ["products", "catalog"]

  - name: orders-service
    host: orders-api.internal
    port: 8080
    protocol: http
    path: /api/v1/orders
    retries: 5
    connect_timeout: 60000
    write_timeout: 60000
    read_timeout: 60000
    tags: ["orders", "transactions"]

# Define Routes
# A Route defines how client requests are routed to a Service.
routes:
  - name: users-route
    protocols: ["http", "https"]
    hosts: ["api.example.com"] # The hostname that Kong will listen for
    paths: ["/users", "/customers"] # Paths that will match this route
    strip_path: true # Remove the matching path prefix before forwarding to the service
    preserve_host: false # Do not preserve the original Host header
    service: users-service # Link this route to the 'users-service' defined above
    tags: ["users", "public"]

  - name: products-route
    protocols: ["http", "https"]
    hosts: ["api.example.com"]
    paths: ["/products"]
    methods: ["GET", "POST"] # Only allow GET and POST methods
    strip_path: true
    preserve_host: false
    service: products-service # Link this route to the 'products-service' which uses the Upstream
    tags: ["products", "public"]

  - name: orders-route
    protocols: ["http", "https"]
    hosts: ["api.example.com"]
    paths: ["/orders"]
    methods: ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"]
    strip_path: true
    preserve_host: false
    service: orders-service
    tags: ["orders", "public"]

  - name: internal-admin-route
    protocols: ["http", "https"]
    hosts: ["admin.example.com"]
    paths: ["/admin/users"]
    strip_path: true
    preserve_host: false
    service: users-service
    tags: ["admin", "internal"] # Example of an internal route for admin access
